data.title: Returns On Capital At Aguas Andinas (SNSE:AGUAS-A) Have Stalled
data.description:
There are a few key trends to look for if we want to identify the next multi-bagger. Firstly, we'd want to identify a growing return on capital employed (ROCE) and then alongside that, an ever-increasing base of capital employed. This shows us that it's a compounding machine, able to continually reinvest its earnings back into the business and generate higher returns. However, after briefly looking over the numbers, we don't think Aguas Andinas (SNSE:AGUAS-A) has the makings of a multi-bagger going forward, but let's have a look at why that may be.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

Return On Capital Employed (ROCE): What Is It?

For those that aren't sure what ROCE is, it measures the amount of pre-tax profits a company can generate from the capital employed in its business. The formula for this calculation on Aguas Andinas is:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.089 = CL$259b ÷ (CL$3.2t - CL$260b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

So, Aguas Andinas has an ROCE of 8.9%. On its own that's a low return on capital but it's in line with the industry's average returns of 8.9%.

See our latest analysis for Aguas Andinas

Above you can see how the current ROCE for Aguas Andinas compares to its prior returns on capital, but there's only so much you can tell from the past. If you'd like, you can check out the forecasts from the analysts covering Aguas Andinas for free.

How Are Returns Trending?

The returns on capital haven't changed much for Aguas Andinas in recent years. Over the past five years, ROCE has remained relatively flat at around 8.9% and the business has deployed 46% more capital into its operations. Given the company has increased the amount of capital employed, it appears the investments that have been made simply don't provide a high return on capital.

What We Can Learn From Aguas Andinas' ROCE

Long story short, while Aguas Andinas has been reinvesting its capital, the returns that it's generating haven't increased. Investors must think there's better things to come because the stock has knocked it out of the park, delivering a 138% gain to shareholders who have held over the last five years. But if the trajectory of these underlying trends continue, we think the likelihood of it being a multi-bagger from here isn't high.

data.title: Cardinal Energy (TSE:CJ) Will Pay A Dividend Of CA$0.06
data.description:
The board of Cardinal Energy Ltd. (TSE:CJ) has announced that it will pay a dividend on the 17th of February, with investors receiving CA$0.06 per share. Based on this payment, the dividend yield on the company's stock will be 7.9%, which is an attractive boost to shareholder returns.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

Cardinal Energy's Projections Indicate Future Payments May Be Unsustainable

Impressive dividend yields are good, but this doesn't matter much if the payments can't be sustained. Before this announcement, Cardinal Energy was paying out 150% of what it was earning, and not generating any free cash flows either. Paying out such a large dividend compared to earnings while also not generating any free cash flow would definitely be difficult to keep up.

Looking forward, earnings per share is forecast to fall by 15.9% over the next year. Assuming the dividend continues along recent trends, we believe the payout ratio could reach 180%, which could put the dividend under pressure if earnings don't start to improve.

View our latest analysis for Cardinal Energy

Dividend Volatility

While the company has been paying a dividend for a long time, it has cut the dividend at least once in the last 10 years. The dividend has gone from an annual total of CA$0.84 in 2016 to the most recent total annual payment of CA$0.72. The dividend has shrunk at around 1.5% a year during that period. A company that decreases its dividend over time generally isn't what we are looking for.

Dividend Growth Could Be Constrained

Growing earnings per share could be a mitigating factor when considering the past fluctuations in the dividend. Cardinal Energy has impressed us by growing EPS at 17% per year over the past five years. However, the company isn't reinvesting a lot back into the business, so we would expect the growth rate to slow down somewhat in the future.

Cardinal Energy's Dividend Doesn't Look Sustainable

In summary, while it's good to see that the dividend hasn't been cut, we are a bit cautious about Cardinal Energy's payments, as there could be some issues with sustaining them into the future. In general, the distributions are a little bit higher than we would like, but we can't ignore the fact the quickly growing earnings gives this stock great potential in the future. We don't think Cardinal Energy is a great stock to add to your portfolio if income is your focus.

Companies possessing a stable dividend policy will likely enjoy greater investor interest than those suffering from a more inconsistent approach. At the same time, there are other factors our readers should be conscious of before pouring capital into a stock. For example, we've identified 3 warning signs for Cardinal Energy (2 make us uncomfortable!) that you should be aware of before investing. data.title: Nvidia Faces China Chip Disruption While Expanding AI And Healthcare Reach
data.description:
• Chinese customs authorities have blocked shipments of Nvidia's H200 AI processors into China, prompting suppliers to pause production and disrupting supply of these chips.
  • CEO Jensen Huang is planning a late January visit to China as trade tensions and export policy adjustments create uncertainty for NasdaqGS:NVDA in this key market.
  • Nvidia is also expanding its AI footprint through new partnerships, including a US$1b co innovation lab with Eli Lilly and collaborations in life sciences and robotics.

Nvidia, traded as NasdaqGS:NVDA, is a central player in AI computing, with its GPUs widely used in data centers, research, and advanced automation. The current supply chain disruption for H200 chips in China coincides with ongoing trade frictions and tighter export rules, directly affecting a region that has been an important outlet for its AI hardware. At the same time, the company is expanding into healthcare and robotics through new AI partnerships that focus on infrastructure and real world applications.

For investors, these developments highlight both operational risk in a major market and potential opportunity in higher value AI ecosystems. Jensen Huang's upcoming China trip underscores the importance of regulatory clarity and business continuity in the region, while the Eli Lilly co innovation lab and other alliances illustrate how Nvidia is positioning its technology across drug discovery, diagnostics, and automation over an extended time frame.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for NVIDIA by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on NVIDIA.

Is NVIDIA financially strong enough to weather the next crisis?

The H200 blockage in China puts a spotlight on how much regulatory friction can affect where Nvidia’s data center revenue comes from, especially given prior expectations for more than 1,000,000 H200 orders in that market. At the same time, Jensen Huang’s China visit and the US$1b Eli Lilly co innovation lab, plus a cluster of new healthcare and robotics collaborations, suggest Nvidia is putting more emphasis on AI ecosystems and geographically diverse demand rather than relying on any single country.

Advertisement

How This Fits, Or Clashes, With The Usual NVIDIA Narrative

Nvidia is often described as an AI infrastructure pure play tied closely to hyperscalers and China exposure, so customs blocks on H200 processors and talk of new US oversight on advanced AI exports directly test that storyline. The recent MoUs and deals with Eli Lilly, Thermo Fisher, Natera, Multiply Labs, Freenome and robotics partners broaden the narrative from one focused mainly on training chips in big tech data centers to one where Nvidia’s hardware and software sit inside regulated healthcare and industrial workflows as well.

Risks and rewards investors should weigh

  • ⚠️ Export controls, tariffs and a potential two year ban on Blackwell class exports to China could limit access to a historically important market and introduce more policy headline risk.
  • ⚠️ H200 shipment halts and customs uncertainty raise the chance of delayed orders, supply chain inefficiencies and pressure to redesign products for specific jurisdictions.
  • 🎁 Healthcare and life sciences alliances, including the co innovation lab with Eli Lilly and expanded BioNeMo offering, create new use cases for Nvidia’s AI stack in large, regulated end markets.
  • 🎁 Partnerships across robotics, quantum links, data center operators and software platforms can deepen Nvidia’s role as a full stack AI provider, not just a chip vendor, which some investors view as a support for more durable revenue streams.

What to watch next

From here, keep an eye on how quickly customs issues around the H200 are clarified, whether any formal Chinese import decision is announced, and how export legislation on advanced chips evolves in the US and key allies. It is also worth tracking deal flow and product adoption inside the new healthcare and robotics partnerships, since consistent contract wins there could offset any regional pressure on data center hardware. You can also stay on top of how these themes feed into sentiment by checking out the latest community views in our AI and semiconductor narratives hub.

data.title: Boeing Wins Key Orders As Recovery And Cash Flow Story Builds
data.description:
• Ethiopian Airlines has placed a major new aircraft order with Boeing (NYSE:BA), adding fresh demand for the company’s widebody and narrowbody jets.
  • Delta Air Lines has also committed to additional Boeing aircraft, expanding its fleet plans with new long haul and domestic capacity.
  • Boeing has overtaken Airbus in net commercial orders for the first time in seven years, supported by higher 787 Dreamliner production rates and increasing long haul demand.

Boeing sits at the center of global air travel, supplying commercial jets to airlines that want to refresh fleets and plan for long term passenger demand. After several challenging years for both the company and the wider aviation industry, fresh commitments from Ethiopian Airlines and Delta Air Lines indicate that airlines are planning for more long haul and international flying.

For you as an investor, these new orders and higher production levels offer a clearer view of how Boeing’s commercial segment is being used by airlines. The key questions now revolve around how consistently Boeing can deliver aircraft, manage its supply chain and convert this order momentum into steady cash generation over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Boeing by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Boeing.

How Boeing stacks up against its biggest competitors

Boeing’s fresh 787 orders from Ethiopian Airlines and Delta, combined with 600 commercial aircraft deliveries in 2025, point to airlines committing capital to its widebody and narrowbody platforms over many years. For you, that matters because these contracts feed Boeing’s backlog, support its manufacturing ramp on the 787 program and provide more visibility on how the commercial business might absorb fixed costs and support future cash generation.

Advertisement

How This News Fits Into The Boeing Narrative

Recent commentary has framed Boeing as a recovery and cash flow story, with investors focused less on near term earnings and more on the company’s ability to stabilize production and deliveries. The new orders, the tie up with Salient Motion to speed up certification of motion control systems and evidence of higher Dreamlifter activity all add detail to that narrative by showing where operational improvements and product demand are intersecting.

Risks and Rewards To Keep In Mind

  • Large 787 and 737 MAX commitments from Delta and Ethiopian add to Boeing’s order book and may support long term production planning.
  • Growing 787 output and partnerships like Salient Motion’s modular actuation platform could help reduce unit costs and shorten development timelines.
  • Boeing still faces scrutiny over manufacturing quality and certification processes, so any setbacks could affect deliveries and customer confidence.
  • Trade and tariff discussions that explicitly reference Boeing aircraft create an external risk that sits outside operational progress.

What To Watch Next

From here, it is worth watching how consistently Boeing converts this order momentum into aircraft deliveries, how the 787 production rate evolves, and whether new technology partnerships actually speed certification and lower costs. Looking ahead, you may want to watch three things in particular: final court approval of the settlement and any follow on conduct requirements; how regulators and courts respond to the proposed 10% cap; and the degree of clarity management provides on Discover integration, card pricing and capital returns on upcoming calls. data.description:
The board of Gulf Warehousing Company Q.P.S.C. (DSM:GWCS) has announced that it will pay a dividend of QAR0.10 per share on the 1st of January. The dividend yield will be 4.3% based on this payment which is still above the industry average.

Gulf Warehousing Company Q.P.S.C's Projected Earnings Seem Likely To Cover Future Distributions

We like to see robust dividend yields, but that doesn't matter if the payment isn't sustainable. Prior to this announcement, Gulf Warehousing Company Q.P.S.C's dividend was comfortably covered by both cash flow and earnings. This means that a large portion of its earnings are being retained to grow the business.

Over the next year, EPS is forecast to expand by 12.8%. Assuming the dividend continues along recent trends, we think the payout ratio could be 41% by next year, which is in a pretty sustainable range.

See our latest analysis for Gulf Warehousing Company Q.P.S.C

Dividend Volatility

Although the company has a long dividend history, it has been cut at least once in the last 10 years. The dividend has gone from an annual total of QAR0.15 in 2016 to the most recent total annual payment of QAR0.10. This works out to be a decline of approximately 4.0% per year over that time. Declining dividends isn't generally what we look for as they can indicate that the company is running into some challenges.

Dividend Growth Potential Is Shaky

With a relatively unstable dividend, it's even more important to see if earnings per share is growing. Earnings per share has been sinking by 13% over the last five years. Such rapid declines definitely have the potential to constrain dividend payments if the trend continues into the future. On the bright side, earnings are predicted to gain some ground over the next year, but until this turns into a pattern we wouldn't be feeling too comfortable.

Our Thoughts On Gulf Warehousing Company Q.P.S.C's Dividend

Overall, it's nice to see a consistent dividend payment, but we think that longer term, the current level of payment might be unsustainable. The company is generating plenty of cash, which could maintain the dividend for a while, but the track record hasn't been great. We don't think Gulf Warehousing Company Q.P.S.C is a great stock to add to your portfolio if income is your focus.

data.title: CTS (NYSE:CTS) Might Have The Makings Of A Multi-Bagger
data.description:
If we want to find a potential multi-bagger, often there are underlying trends that can provide clues. Amongst other things, we'll want to see two things; firstly, a growing return on capital employed (ROCE) and secondly, an expansion in the company's amount of capital employed. Ultimately, this demonstrates that it's a business that is reinvesting profits at increasing rates of return. So on that note, CTS (NYSE:CTS) looks quite promising in regards to its trends of return on capital.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

Understanding Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)

For those that aren't sure what ROCE is, it measures the amount of pre-tax profits a company can generate from the capital employed in its business. Analysts use this formula to calculate it for CTS:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.12 = US$81m ÷ (US$790m - US$105m) (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

Therefore, CTS has an ROCE of 12%. In absolute terms, that's a satisfactory return, but compared to the Electronic industry average of 9.0% it's much better.

Check out our latest analysis for CTS

In the above chart we have measured CTS' prior ROCE against its prior performance, but the future is arguably more important. If you'd like, you can check out the forecasts from the analysts covering CTS for free.

What The Trend Of ROCE Can Tell Us

We like the trends that we're seeing from CTS. Over the last five years, returns on capital employed have risen substantially to 12%. The company is effectively making more money per dollar of capital used, and it's worth noting that the amount of capital has increased too, by 22%. The increasing returns on a growing amount of capital is common amongst multi-baggers and that's why we're impressed.

Our Take On CTS' ROCE

In summary, it's great to see that CTS can compound returns by consistently reinvesting capital at increasing rates of return, because these are some of the key ingredients of those highly sought after multi-baggers. And investors seem to expect more of this going forward, since the stock has rewarded shareholders with a 69% return over the last five years. With that being said, we still think the promising fundamentals mean the company deserves some further due diligence.

data.title: Here's Why Randstad (AMS:RAND) Can Manage Its Debt Responsibly
data.description:
The external fund manager backed by Berkshire Hathaway's Charlie Munger, Li Lu, makes no bones about it when he says 'The biggest investment risk is not the volatility of prices, but whether you will suffer a permanent loss of capital.' So it seems the smart money knows that debt - which is usually involved in bankruptcies - is a very important factor, when you assess how risky a company is. As with many other companies Randstad N.V. (AMS:RAND) makes use of debt. But is this debt a concern to shareholders?

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

When Is Debt Dangerous?

Generally speaking, debt only becomes a real problem when a company can't easily pay it off, either by raising capital or with its own cash flow. Ultimately, if the company can't fulfill its legal obligations to repay debt, shareholders could walk away with nothing. While that is not too common, we often do see indebted companies permanently diluting shareholders because lenders force them to raise capital at a distressed price. By replacing dilution, though, debt can be an extremely good tool for businesses that need capital to invest in growth at high rates of return. When we examine debt levels, we first consider both cash and debt levels, together.

What Is Randstad's Debt?

The image below, which you can click on for greater detail, shows that at September 2025 Randstad had debt of €1.52b, up from €1.24b in one year. However, it also had €295.0m in cash, and so its net debt is €1.23b.

How Strong Is Randstad's Balance Sheet?

The latest balance sheet data shows that Randstad had liabilities of €4.91b due within a year, and liabilities of €1.90b falling due after that. Offsetting this, it had €295.0m in cash and €5.51b in receivables that were due within 12 months. So its liabilities total €1.01b more than the combination of its cash and short-term receivables.

While this might seem like a lot, it is not so bad since Randstad has a market capitalization of €5.00b, and so it could probably strengthen its balance sheet by raising capital if it needed to. But it's clear that we should definitely closely examine whether it can manage its debt without dilution.

Check out our latest analysis for Randstad

We measure a company's debt load relative to its earnings power by looking at its net debt divided by its earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) and by calculating how easily its earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) cover its interest expense (interest cover). This way, we consider both the absolute quantum of the debt, as well as the interest rates paid on it.

Randstad has net debt worth 2.0 times EBITDA, which isn't too much, but its interest cover looks a bit on the low side, with EBIT at only 5.1 times the interest expense. While these numbers do not alarm us, it's worth noting that the cost of the company's debt is having a real impact. Shareholders should be aware that Randstad's EBIT was down 22% last year. If that earnings trend continues then paying off its debt will be about as easy as herding cats on to a roller coaster. The balance sheet is clearly the area to focus on when you are analysing debt. But ultimately the future profitability of the business will decide if Randstad can strengthen its balance sheet over time. So if you want to see what the professionals think, you might find this free report on analyst profit forecasts to be interesting.

Finally, while the tax-man may adore accounting profits, lenders only accept cold hard cash. So we clearly need to look at whether that EBIT is leading to corresponding free cash flow. Over the last three years, Randstad actually produced more free cash flow than EBIT. That sort of strong cash conversion gets us as excited as the crowd when the beat drops at a Daft Punk concert.

Our View

Randstad's EBIT growth rate was a real negative on this analysis, although the other factors we considered were considerably better. In particular, we are dazzled with its conversion of EBIT to free cash flow. Looking at all this data makes us feel a little cautious about Randstad's debt levels. While debt does have its upside in higher potential returns, we think shareholders should definitely consider how debt levels might make the stock more risky. There's no doubt that we learn most about debt from the balance sheet. However, not all investment risk resides within the balance sheet - far from it. Case in point: We've spotted 3 warning signs for Randstad you should be aware of.
